revision.comment: Auto-generated page using script written by Immanuelle, either as a placeholder redirect or to make bots recognize the page as made by Immanuelle
revision.text: #redirect[[en:Kimata-no-Kami]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]
木俣神は大穴遅神が八上比売に生ませた御子。 須勢理比売を正妻に迎えたことで須勢理比売の嫉妬の鉾先が自分に向けられるのを恐れて、八上比売は御子の木俣神を木の根元の部分に捨てていったことより名がついた。 亦の名を御井神とも言う。 木俣神は木の神とも言われる。
木俣神は大穴遅神が八上比売に生ませた御子。 須勢理比売を正妻に迎えたことで須勢理比売の嫉妬の鉾先が自分に向けられるのを恐れて、八上比売は御子の木俣神を木の根元の部分に捨てていったことより名がついた。 またの名を御井神とも言う。 木俣神は木の神とも言われる。

[[画像:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|大国主の系図（古事記による）。青は男神、赤は女神、黄は性別不詳]]'''木俣神'''（きのまたのかみ、このまたのかみ）は[[日本神話]]の中で、[[大国主|大穴牟遅神]]が因幡の八上比売に生ませた[[神]]。八上比売は大穴牟遅神の最初の妻であったが、[[スセリビメ|須勢理毘売]]を正妻に迎えたため、これを恐れ、子を木の俣に刺し挟んで実家に帰ってしまった。そのため、その子を名づけて木俣神という。またの名を'''御井神'''（みいのかみ）という。『[[古事記]]』では性別不詳であるが、祭神としている各神社の社伝では、大穴牟遅神の長男としている例が多い。一般的に木の神、水神、安産の神として崇敬されている。
== 主な神社 == * [[御井神社 (斐川町)|御井神社]]（[[島根県]][[斐川町]]） * [[御井神社 (養父市)|御井神社]]（[[兵庫県]][[養父市]]） * [[御井神社 (宇陀市)|御井神社]]（[[奈良県]][[宇陀市]]） * [[津田神社]]（[[三重県]][[多気町]]） * [[五百井神社]]（[[滋賀県]][[栗東市]]） * [[気多若宮神社]]（[[岐阜県]][[飛騨市]]） * [[大井神社]]（[[京都府]][[亀岡市]]）
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の神の一覧]] * [[大国主]] * [[大国主の神話]] * [[スセリビメ]]
{{shinto-stub}} [[Category:日本の神|きのまた]]

八上比売は大穴牟遅神の最初の妻であったが、[[スセリビメ|須勢理毘売]]を正妻に迎えたため、これを恐れ、子を木の俣に刺し挟んで実家に帰ってしまった。そのため、その子を名づけて木俣神という。またの名を'''御井神'''（みいのかみ）という。
『[[古事記]]』では性別不詳であるが、祭神としている各神社の社伝では、大穴牟遅神の長男としている例が多い。一般的に木の神、水神、安産の神として崇敬されている。
== 主な神社 == * [[御井神社 (斐川町)|御井神社]]（[[島根県]][[出雲市]]） * [[御井神社 (養父市)|御井神社]]（[[兵庫県]][[養父市]]） * [[御井神社 (宇陀市)|御井神社]]（[[奈良県]][[宇陀市]]） * [[津田神社]]（[[三重県]][[多気町]]） * [[五百井神社]]（[[滋賀県]][[栗東市]]） * [[気多若宮神社]]（[[岐阜県]][[飛騨市]]） * [[御井神社 (各務原市)|御井神社]]（[[岐阜県]][[各務原市]]） * [[御井神社 (養老町)|御井神社]]（岐阜県[[養老郡]][[養老町]]） * [[大井神社 (亀岡市)|大井神社]]（[[京都府]][[亀岡市]]）

revision.text:
[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

revision.text:
[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

revision.text:
[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseribime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== Related Topics == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "大穴牟遅神}}" to "大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}}"
revision.text:
[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}
{{Shinto 横}} {{Shinto-stub}}

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

revision.text:
[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

revision.text:
[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

revision.text:
[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})
== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})

Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.

revision.text:
[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}
{{Shinto 横}} {{Shinto-stub}}

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}})== See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}}) == See Also! == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "== See Also! ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}}) == See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}
{{Shinto 横}} {{Shinto-stub}}

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}}) == See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}}) == See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}

revision.text:
[[ja:木俣神]]
[[File:Ohokuninushi family tree.png|thumb|right|250px|Genealogy of Ōkuninushi (according to the Kojiki). Blue indicates male deities, red indicates female deities, and yellow indicates unspecified gender.]] '''Kimata no Kami''' (木俣神, きまたのかみ, also read as Kinomata no Kami or Konomata no Kami) is a deity in Japanese mythology, born to {{ill|Ōnamuchi no Mikoto|ja|大穴牟遅神|1=Ōkuninushi|lt=Ōnamuchi}} and {{ill|Yakami Hime|ja|八上比売|1=Yagami-hime}}.
Yakami Hime was the first wife of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto, but she feared {{ill|Suseri-Hime|ja|須勢理毘売}}, who became Ōnamuchi’s principal wife. Consequently, she left her child in the fork of a tree and returned to her parents' home. The child was thus named "Kimata no Kami," meaning "Deity of the Tree Fork." The deity is also known by the name '''Mii no Kami''' (御井神).
In the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, the gender of Kimata no Kami is not specified, but shrine traditions generally identify the deity as the eldest son of Ōnamuchi no Mikoto. Kimata no Kami is widely revered as a deity of trees, water, and safe childbirth.
== Major Shrines == * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Izumo)|ja|御井神社 (出雲市)}} ({{ill|Izumo|ja|島根県出雲市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yabu)|ja|御井神社 (養父市)}} ({{ill|Yabu|ja|兵庫県養父市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Uda)|ja|御井神社 (宇陀市)}} ({{ill|Uda|ja|奈良県宇陀市}}) * {{ill|Tsuda Shrine|ja|津田神社}} ({{ill|Taki|ja|三重県多気町}}) * {{ill|Ioi Shrine|ja|五百井神社}} ({{ill|Ritto|ja|滋賀県栗東市}}) * {{ill|Keta Wakamiya Shrine|ja|気多若宮神社}} ({{ill|Hida|ja|岐阜県飛騨市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Kakamigahara)|ja|御井神社 (各務原市)}} ({{ill|Kakamigahara|ja|岐阜県各務原市}}) * {{ill|Mii Shrine (Yōrō)|ja|御井神社 (養老町)}} ({{ill|Yōrō|ja|岐阜県養老郡養老町}}) * {{ill|Ōi Shrine (Kameoka)|ja|大井神社 (亀岡市)}} ({{ill|Kameoka|ja|京都府亀岡市}}) == See Also == * {{ill|List of Japanese deities|ja|日本の神の一覧}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主}} * {{ill|Ōkuninushi’s myths|ja|大国主の神話}}
